title: Cost and profit negotiations - Analyze and negotiate profit | CanadaBuys

breadcrumbs.title: Getting started with cost and profit negotiations

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: Analyze and negotiate profit

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: The second step in building a negotiated contract price is determining the profit.

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: The total profit is the maximum profit amount that should be awarded under the Profit Principles for negotiated, cost-based pricing (Section 5.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing ).

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: Profit Determination Template as found in the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing Annex 5.2 illustrates the Profit Determination Process including all components of the profit described in this section.

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: Profit levels will vary to recognize:

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: the cost of money associated with the capital employed by the contractor in performing the contract

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: the levels of general business risk

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: the levels of contractual risk assumed by the contractor in performing the contract

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: The total negotiated contract profit is calculated by adding the profit amounts for three factors:

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: return on capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: general business risk

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: contractual risk

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: Total Profit = Return on Capital Employed + General Business Risk + Contractual Risk

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: The following conditions must be respected when calculating total profit:

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: the total allowable profit must be the lower of:

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: the sum of supportable amounts by factor, or

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: 16% of the total cost

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: the total amount of profit awarded under all the above factors must not exceed 16% of the total contract costs

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: the profit for each factor should be calculated separately and included in the price of each line item with a distinct basis of payment in the contract

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: The maximum contract profit rate will not exceed 16% of total contract costs. This represents the maximum profit threshold reflecting the highest risk and rare fixed price contract scenarios with significant Capital Employed, extreme General Business Risk (primarily Direct Labour and Overhead costs) and maximum Contractual Risk.

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: While incentives are not included in the Profit Principles determination of the negotiated contract profit, it’s essential to understand the impact of incentives in a procurement on the overall contract price. The total contract price consists of the total contract costs/estimated costs, profit, and incentives. Therefore, the outcome is potentially an increased contract price and overall profit and earnings for the contractor.

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: Subcontract costs included in the contract costs will also have a degree of profit included in the costs that are submitted to Canada. Each subcontractor in the supply chain is compensated for costs incurred plus a profit. Profit is layered into subcontract costs at each step in the supply chain. So, where possible, it is important to be aware of the degree or level of profit awarded to subcontractors, as Canada then includes a final layer of profit on the total through the general business and contractual risk profit factors. Transparency and clarity on the total cumulative profit within the supply chain is important where possible to understand and assess for reasonability and added value. Related party transactions are another area where profit layering can happen. For information specifically on related party transactions, refer to section 5.1.3.1 Transfer Pricing of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing .

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: Before price negotiations with the offeror/contractor, you must:

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: obtain from the offeror/contractor a schedule estimating the working capital employed and the fixed capital employed in the contract, using the Profit Determination Template

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: ensure the template is completed and that you have the supporting documentation for the figures presented in the template

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: review the Profit Determination Template for accuracy and reasonability, with the support of a price advisor, in advance of the price negotiation

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: Note : You will make the final determination of whether the profit is fair and reasonable.

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: Profit Principles apply to all negotiated contracts, with the following exceptions:

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: low dollar value contracts valued under $50,000

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: contracts, or parts of contracts, with commercial pricing such as catalogues, price lists or fee schedules, where only discounts are subject to negotiation

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: contracts with pricing based on alternative strategies

structure_blocks.heading: Analyze and negotiate profit
structure_blocks.text: Refer to the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2, Profit Principles for more information.

structure_blocks.heading: Capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The capital employed component of profit recognizes the capital investment required to fulfill a contract and provide a reasonable return, regardless of how a contractor is financed.

structure_blocks.heading: Capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The return on capital employed consists of two parts:

structure_blocks.heading: Capital employed
structure_blocks.text: return on working capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Capital employed
structure_blocks.text: return on fixed capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Applicable rates can be found in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table .

structure_blocks.heading: Capital employed
structure_blocks.text: If the profit determination is related to previous periods, applicable rates for those periods must be used. If at the time of contract award, the relevant rates have increased or decreased by more than one full point, the return will be recalculated using the revised rates.

structure_blocks.heading: Capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The following clause must be included in the price proposal, after consultation with a price advisor:

structure_blocks.heading: Capital employed
structure_blocks.text: “The price quoted includes an amount of profit using the 3-year rolling average [insert name of appropriate rate] of [insert appropriate rate] percent. In the event that the [insert name of appropriate rate] at the time of contract award has changed by more than one full point, up or down from the previous year, after consultation with the Price Advisory Group, the price will be adjusted to reflect the applicable rate.”

structure_blocks.heading: Capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Refer to the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2.1, Negotiated Profit Element: Capital Employed for more information.

structure_blocks.heading: Return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Return on working capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Working capital employed refers to the costs of maintaining daily operations within an organization. A contractor makes working capital investments to fund the day-to-day contractual operations, whether it is with current assets, such as cash and investments or with current liabilities, such as loans and accounts payable.

structure_blocks.heading: Return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: There are two approaches for calculating the return on working capital employed, based on the degree of working capital investment in a contract. The two-tiered approach provides a simplified approach for contracts with lower dollar costs and a full schedule approach for contracts with higher costs.

structure_blocks.heading: Return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: For detailed calculations, considerations and examples for Return on Working Capital Employed, see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2.1.1, Return on Working Capital Employed.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 1 return on working capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 1 is an optional, simplified approach that can only be used for contracts with total estimated or acceptable contract costs less than or equal to $1,000,000.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The Tier 1 formula is:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Return on Working Capital Employed = 1-year Guaranteed Investment Certificate (GIC) Rate × Total Acceptable Contract Cost

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: where:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: 1-Year GIC Rate :

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: A 3-year rolling average of the 1-Year GIC Rate is applied in Tier 1 Working Capital Employed calculations.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: This rate, sourced from the Bank of Canada, can be found in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table in Annex 5.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: If profit determination is related to previous periods, applicable rates during the same periods must be used

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: In the event that the relevant rates at the time of contract award have changed by more than one full point, up or down, the return will be recomputed applying the revised rates

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The following clause must be included in the price proposal, after consultation with the Price Advisory Group:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: “The price quoted includes an amount of profit using the 3-year rolling average 1-Year GIC Rate of (insert appropriate rate) percent. In the event that the annual 1-Year GIC Rate at the time of contract award has changed by more than one full point, up or down from the previous year, after consultation with the Price Advisory Group, the price will be adjusted to reflect the applicable rate.”

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Total Acceptable Contract Cost:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The total estimated acceptable contract costs applied as the base against which the 3-year average GIC rate is applied, representing the maximum working capital a contractor would have employed in the contract.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 2 return on working capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 2 is a full schedule approach for contracts with total estimated or acceptable contract costs over $1,000,000. It can also be used for Tier 1 contracts upon request from the offeror/contractor.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Refer to Tier 2 - Working Capital tab of the Profit Determination Template for more information.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The Tier 2 formula is:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Return on Working Capital Employed = Bank Prime Rate / 12 × Sum of Cumulative Monthly Working Capital Employed

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: where:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Bank Prime Rate / 12:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The 3-year average bank prime rate applicable to the contract for the Tier 2 Working Capital calculation.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: This rate, sourced from the Bank of Canada, can be found in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table in Annex 5.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: If profit determination is related to previous periods, applicable rates during the same periods must be used

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: In the event that the relevant rates at the time of contract award have changed by more than one full point, up or down, the return will be recomputed applying the revised rates

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: You must consult with the Price Advisory Group to ensure that the appropriate clause is included in the price proposal

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Cumulative Working Capital Employed:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Contractors must submit a schedule of estimated monthly net working capital for the contract, using the Return on Working Capital Employed Tab found in the Profit Determination Template in the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing . In order to prepare and review this schedule, the following information is required:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Monthly Estimated Net Working Capital:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The schedule of acceptable contract costs, exclusive of depreciation (per the contract clauses and conditions, Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 and the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing Annex 2, Costing Standard)

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The schedule of contract revenue payments, exclusive of profit (payment plan/schedule)

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Advance payments must be applied in the full schedule

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Contracts with advance payments and progress payments do not require a return on working capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Calculation methodology: The 3-year average bank prime rate, as defined above, is an annual rate of return. The calculation below accumulates the monthly working capital return, and as such, the 3-year average bank prime rate is divided by 12 to reflect the monthly earning. The return on the cumulative working capital employed is calculated as follows:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on working capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Total : Bank Prime Rate / 12 × Monthly Cumulative Working Capital

structure_blocks.heading: Return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Return on fixed capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Fixed capital employed consists of the investments contractors make that the company will use for more than one year. Fixed costs are typically purchased for use in the current and future years.

structure_blocks.heading: Return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The investment in fixed capital is capitalized, allowing the contractor to spread out the cost over the asset's useful life. Contractors are required to invest in fixed capital for the purposes of carrying out contracts and the fixed capital can often be used for one or multiple contracts.

structure_blocks.heading: Return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: For more calculations, considerations and examples for Return on Fixed Capital Employed, refer to the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2.1.2, Return on Fixed Capital Employed.

structure_blocks.heading: Return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The provision of a return on fixed capital employed is intended not only to compensate contractors for the cost of money associated with the fixed capital employed on the contract, but also to encourage investment in new capital equipment, the result of which is generally greater productivity and consequently reduced costs to Canada.

structure_blocks.heading: Return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: For the purpose of this section, the fixed capital employed is defined as the net book value of fixed assets, less:

structure_blocks.heading: Return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: land and any intangible assets

structure_blocks.heading: Return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: any fixed assets not in use such as idle plants

structure_blocks.heading: Return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: any surplus value arising from re-appraisal

structure_blocks.heading: Return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: There are three approaches for calculating the return on fixed capital employed, based on the total costs and the degree of fixed capital investment in a contract. This three-tiered approach is as follows:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 1 return on fixed capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 1 is a simplified, optional approach for all contracts with total estimated or acceptable costs less than or equal to $1,000,000. The Tier 1 formula is:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Return on Fixed Capital Employed = 1% × Total Acceptable Contract Cost

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 1 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 1 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: If machinery and/or equipment owned by the contractor is regularly used in the manufacture of the product(s), or in the provision of services, under the contract, a return on fixed capital employed of 1% of total allowable costs will be awarded.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 2 is a simplified, optional approach for:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Contracts with total estimated or acceptable costs less than or equal to $20,000,000

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Contracts with lower fixed capital intensity level, such as services contracts with minimal fixed capital needs

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 1 contracts upon the offeror’s/contractor’s request

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The Tier 2 formula is:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Return on Fixed Capital Employed = Capital Intensity Rate × ( Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index (CPI) Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead ) × Total Acceptable Contract Cost

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: where:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Capital Intensity Rate:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Posted in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Annex 5.1, is applied to the Total Acceptable Contract Costs to estimate the fixed capital employed for contracts with lower levels of fixed capital investments. This rate is established annually based on a review of fixed capital employed calculations on a sample of contracts to best reflect a lower level of capital intensity.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: This rate is a 3-year average of the combination of the government marketable bond over 10-year rate with CPI inflation rate 6–10 years ahead.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Total Acceptable Contract Cost:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The base against which the Capital Intensity Rate and Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate with CPI inflation rate 6–10 years are applied.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 3 is a full schedule approach.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 3 is used for:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Contracts with total estimated or acceptable contract costs greater than $1,000,000

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Contracts that do not meet the criteria for Tier 1 and Tier 2

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Tier 1 or Tier 2 contracts upon the offeror’s/contractor’s request

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The Tier 3 formula is:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Return on Fixed Capital Employed = ( Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead ) × Fixed Capital Employed

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: where:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: A 3-year average of the combination of the government marketable bond over 10-year rate with CPI inflation rate 6–10 years ahead.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: Fixed Capital Employed:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: The percentage of the contractor's fixed capital to be used in executing the contract. It is determined by calculating the allocation of the portion of the net book value of assets to be employed in the contract performance by applying the applicable overhead recovery rates (overhead recovery base / total budget amount of recovery base) for the contract to the applicable assets, as detailed in the Profit Determination Template in the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Annex 5.2. This allocation methodology is required unless a contract represents 100% of an organization’s fixed capital use.

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: When the contract period extends over more than one of the contractor's fiscal years, the determination of fixed capital employed must be done for each fiscal year within the contract period. The process outlined above applies for the contractor’s first fiscal year in the contract. To determine the fixed capital employed in subsequent years, the following process applies:

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: the contractor’s net book value of applicable fixed assets is estimated for each fiscal year subsequent to the first year of the contract

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: the estimated overhead recovery factors for each fiscal year, determined via the contract costing rate negotiations are applied for the allocation of the capital assets

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: the fixed capital employed for the contract is the sum of the fixed capital employed for each fiscal year of the contractor within the duration of the contract

structure_blocks.heading: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
heading_path: Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed
structure_blocks.text: the government marketable bond over 10-year rate with CPI inflation rate 6-10 years ahead is applied to the total fixed capital employed in the contract

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: General business risk

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: General business risk recognizes the contractor’s level of effort and its responsibility and risk in managing the resources required to execute the contract efficiently and economically.

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: The return on general business risk, along with the level of effort, responsibility and risk, varies with the nature of the costs and the different cost elements of a contract. This is reflected in the following rates of profit to be applied to the total actual or estimated costs of each element below:

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: Cost elements and rates of profit | Columns: Cost element | Percentage

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: Cost element: Direct materials | Percentage: 1.5%

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: Cost element: Subcontracts | Percentage: 2%

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: Cost element: Accountable advance spares embodied | Percentage: 2%

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: Cost element: Direct labour | Percentage: 4%

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: Cost element: Overhead | Percentage: 4%

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: Cost element: All other allowable costs | Percentage: 1.5%

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: Cost element: Pass through costs | Percentage: 0%

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: The costs for each element is multiplied by its associated percentage. The sum of these calculated amounts equals the total general business risk profit.

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: For more on General Business Risk, including cost element definition, intention, examples, calculations, see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2, Negotiated Profit Element: General Business Risk.

structure_blocks.heading: General business risk
structure_blocks.text: For guidance on calculating rates of profit for General Business Risk, refer to the Profit Determination Template in Annex 5.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing .

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Contractual risk

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Contractual risk represents the likelihood of financial loss to the contractor due to contract-specific factors. Assessing contractual risk involves understanding the risks present within the contract and understanding which party is best to carry the risk for the various components of the contract. The level of profit on contractual risk is based on how much of the risk is carried by the contractor. A higher profit rate for contractual risk, is the result of the risk being assigned to and borne by the contractor. It is important to ensure the assignment of risk is clear, transparent and well-defined.

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: As shown in the table below, the degree of financial risk taken on by a contractor is dependent on the contract basis of payment, which determines the standard (the lowest rate in the range) and the available range of profit:

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Contractor financial risk based on payment type | Columns: Basis of payment | Standard rate | Maximum rate

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Basis of payment: Fixed price or firm price | Standard rate: 4% | Maximum rate: 7%

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Basis of payment: Fixed or firm rate type: ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 4.5%

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Basis of payment: Fixed or firm rate type: No ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 3.5%

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with fixed fee: ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 4.5%

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with fixed fee: No ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 0% | Maximum rate: 1%

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 4.5%

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with no fee or fee based on actual costs | Standard rate: 0% | Maximum rate: 0%

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Note : The percentage can be lower than the standard rate if the nature of the work involves little to no risk.

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Once the standard contractual risk profit rate is determined, the level of contractual risk profit that varies within the ranges depends on the presence, likelihood and impact of the following key risk factors:

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Accuracy of costing : which affects the risk that the costing estimates are not accurate and the contract will cost more than planned, and includes:

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: how well the scope, requirements and demand are established

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: the reliability of cost estimates

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: potential fluctuations from external factors

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: the timing of negotiations

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Technical and schedule factors : which affect the risk that contract-specific factors and requirements will impact a contractor’s ability to successfully complete the contract, and include:

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: technical risk

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: familiarity and program (products/services/processes/tasks) maturity

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: performance specifications

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: contract duration

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Justification must be provided for the total contractual risk profit percentage. You may use the Contractual Risk Assessment Tool to calculate the contractual risk profit percentage and document decisions.

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: The contractual risk profit rate is applied to the total estimated contract costs to develop the contract price. The contract risk profit formula is:

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: Total Contractual Risk Profit = Contractual Risk Rate % × Total Contract Costs

structure_blocks.heading: Contractual risk
structure_blocks.text: For more on Contractual Risk, including definitions, intension, examples and calculations, see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2.3, Negotiated Profit Element: Contractual Risk.

raw_text:
Analyze and negotiate profit

The second step in building a negotiated contract price is determining the profit.

The total profit is the maximum profit amount that should be awarded under the Profit Principles for negotiated, cost-based pricing (Section 5.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing ).

Profit Determination Template as found in the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing Annex 5.2 illustrates the Profit Determination Process including all components of the profit described in this section.

Profit levels will vary to recognize:

the cost of money associated with the capital employed by the contractor in performing the contract

the levels of general business risk

the levels of contractual risk assumed by the contractor in performing the contract

The total negotiated contract profit is calculated by adding the profit amounts for three factors:

return on capital employed

general business risk

contractual risk

Total Profit = Return on Capital Employed + General Business Risk + Contractual Risk

The following conditions must be respected when calculating total profit:

the total allowable profit must be the lower of:

the sum of supportable amounts by factor, or

16% of the total cost

the total amount of profit awarded under all the above factors must not exceed 16% of the total contract costs

the profit for each factor should be calculated separately and included in the price of each line item with a distinct basis of payment in the contract

The maximum contract profit rate will not exceed 16% of total contract costs. This represents the maximum profit threshold reflecting the highest risk and rare fixed price contract scenarios with significant Capital Employed, extreme General Business Risk (primarily Direct Labour and Overhead costs) and maximum Contractual Risk.

While incentives are not included in the Profit Principles determination of the negotiated contract profit, it’s essential to understand the impact of incentives in a procurement on the overall contract price. The total contract price consists of the total contract costs/estimated costs, profit, and incentives. Therefore, the outcome is potentially an increased contract price and overall profit and earnings for the contractor.

Subcontract costs included in the contract costs will also have a degree of profit included in the costs that are submitted to Canada. Each subcontractor in the supply chain is compensated for costs incurred plus a profit. Profit is layered into subcontract costs at each step in the supply chain. So, where possible, it is important to be aware of the degree or level of profit awarded to subcontractors, as Canada then includes a final layer of profit on the total through the general business and contractual risk profit factors. Transparency and clarity on the total cumulative profit within the supply chain is important where possible to understand and assess for reasonability and added value. Related party transactions are another area where profit layering can happen. For information specifically on related party transactions, refer to section 5.1.3.1 Transfer Pricing of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing .

Before price negotiations with the offeror/contractor, you must:

obtain from the offeror/contractor a schedule estimating the working capital employed and the fixed capital employed in the contract, using the Profit Determination Template

ensure the template is completed and that you have the supporting documentation for the figures presented in the template

review the Profit Determination Template for accuracy and reasonability, with the support of a price advisor, in advance of the price negotiation

Note : You will make the final determination of whether the profit is fair and reasonable.

Profit Principles apply to all negotiated contracts, with the following exceptions:

low dollar value contracts valued under $50,000

contracts, or parts of contracts, with commercial pricing such as catalogues, price lists or fee schedules, where only discounts are subject to negotiation

contracts with pricing based on alternative strategies

Refer to the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2, Profit Principles for more information.

Capital employed

The capital employed component of profit recognizes the capital investment required to fulfill a contract and provide a reasonable return, regardless of how a contractor is financed.

The return on capital employed consists of two parts:

return on working capital employed

return on fixed capital employed

Applicable rates can be found in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table .

If the profit determination is related to previous periods, applicable rates for those periods must be used. If at the time of contract award, the relevant rates have increased or decreased by more than one full point, the return will be recalculated using the revised rates.

The following clause must be included in the price proposal, after consultation with a price advisor:

“The price quoted includes an amount of profit using the 3-year rolling average [insert name of appropriate rate] of [insert appropriate rate] percent. In the event that the [insert name of appropriate rate] at the time of contract award has changed by more than one full point, up or down from the previous year, after consultation with the Price Advisory Group, the price will be adjusted to reflect the applicable rate.”

Refer to the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2.1, Negotiated Profit Element: Capital Employed for more information.

Return on working capital employed

Working capital employed refers to the costs of maintaining daily operations within an organization. A contractor makes working capital investments to fund the day-to-day contractual operations, whether it is with current assets, such as cash and investments or with current liabilities, such as loans and accounts payable.

There are two approaches for calculating the return on working capital employed, based on the degree of working capital investment in a contract. The two-tiered approach provides a simplified approach for contracts with lower dollar costs and a full schedule approach for contracts with higher costs.

For detailed calculations, considerations and examples for Return on Working Capital Employed, see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2.1.1, Return on Working Capital Employed.

Tier 1 return on working capital employed

Tier 1 is an optional, simplified approach that can only be used for contracts with total estimated or acceptable contract costs less than or equal to $1,000,000.

The Tier 1 formula is:

Return on Working Capital Employed = 1-year Guaranteed Investment Certificate (GIC) Rate × Total Acceptable Contract Cost

where:

1-Year GIC Rate :

A 3-year rolling average of the 1-Year GIC Rate is applied in Tier 1 Working Capital Employed calculations.

This rate, sourced from the Bank of Canada, can be found in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table in Annex 5.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing

If profit determination is related to previous periods, applicable rates during the same periods must be used

In the event that the relevant rates at the time of contract award have changed by more than one full point, up or down, the return will be recomputed applying the revised rates

The following clause must be included in the price proposal, after consultation with the Price Advisory Group:

“The price quoted includes an amount of profit using the 3-year rolling average 1-Year GIC Rate of (insert appropriate rate) percent. In the event that the annual 1-Year GIC Rate at the time of contract award has changed by more than one full point, up or down from the previous year, after consultation with the Price Advisory Group, the price will be adjusted to reflect the applicable rate.”

Total Acceptable Contract Cost:

The total estimated acceptable contract costs applied as the base against which the 3-year average GIC rate is applied, representing the maximum working capital a contractor would have employed in the contract.

Tier 2 return on working capital employed

Tier 2 is a full schedule approach for contracts with total estimated or acceptable contract costs over $1,000,000. It can also be used for Tier 1 contracts upon request from the offeror/contractor.

Refer to Tier 2 - Working Capital tab of the Profit Determination Template for more information.

The Tier 2 formula is:

Return on Working Capital Employed = Bank Prime Rate / 12 × Sum of Cumulative Monthly Working Capital Employed

where:

Bank Prime Rate / 12:

The 3-year average bank prime rate applicable to the contract for the Tier 2 Working Capital calculation.

This rate, sourced from the Bank of Canada, can be found in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table in Annex 5.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing

If profit determination is related to previous periods, applicable rates during the same periods must be used

In the event that the relevant rates at the time of contract award have changed by more than one full point, up or down, the return will be recomputed applying the revised rates

You must consult with the Price Advisory Group to ensure that the appropriate clause is included in the price proposal

Cumulative Working Capital Employed:

Contractors must submit a schedule of estimated monthly net working capital for the contract, using the Return on Working Capital Employed Tab found in the Profit Determination Template in the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing . In order to prepare and review this schedule, the following information is required:

Monthly Estimated Net Working Capital:

The schedule of acceptable contract costs, exclusive of depreciation (per the contract clauses and conditions, Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 and the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing Annex 2, Costing Standard)

The schedule of contract revenue payments, exclusive of profit (payment plan/schedule)

Advance payments must be applied in the full schedule

Contracts with advance payments and progress payments do not require a return on working capital employed

Calculation methodology: The 3-year average bank prime rate, as defined above, is an annual rate of return. The calculation below accumulates the monthly working capital return, and as such, the 3-year average bank prime rate is divided by 12 to reflect the monthly earning. The return on the cumulative working capital employed is calculated as follows:

Total : Bank Prime Rate / 12 × Monthly Cumulative Working Capital

Return on fixed capital employed

Fixed capital employed consists of the investments contractors make that the company will use for more than one year. Fixed costs are typically purchased for use in the current and future years.

The investment in fixed capital is capitalized, allowing the contractor to spread out the cost over the asset's useful life. Contractors are required to invest in fixed capital for the purposes of carrying out contracts and the fixed capital can often be used for one or multiple contracts.

For more calculations, considerations and examples for Return on Fixed Capital Employed, refer to the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2.1.2, Return on Fixed Capital Employed.

The provision of a return on fixed capital employed is intended not only to compensate contractors for the cost of money associated with the fixed capital employed on the contract, but also to encourage investment in new capital equipment, the result of which is generally greater productivity and consequently reduced costs to Canada.

For the purpose of this section, the fixed capital employed is defined as the net book value of fixed assets, less:

land and any intangible assets

any fixed assets not in use such as idle plants

any surplus value arising from re-appraisal

There are three approaches for calculating the return on fixed capital employed, based on the total costs and the degree of fixed capital investment in a contract. This three-tiered approach is as follows:

Tier 1 return on fixed capital employed

Tier 1 is a simplified, optional approach for all contracts with total estimated or acceptable costs less than or equal to $1,000,000. The Tier 1 formula is:

Return on Fixed Capital Employed = 1% × Total Acceptable Contract Cost

If machinery and/or equipment owned by the contractor is regularly used in the manufacture of the product(s), or in the provision of services, under the contract, a return on fixed capital employed of 1% of total allowable costs will be awarded.

Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed

Tier 2 is a simplified, optional approach for:

Contracts with total estimated or acceptable costs less than or equal to $20,000,000

Contracts with lower fixed capital intensity level, such as services contracts with minimal fixed capital needs

Tier 1 contracts upon the offeror’s/contractor’s request

The Tier 2 formula is:

Return on Fixed Capital Employed = Capital Intensity Rate × ( Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index (CPI) Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead ) × Total Acceptable Contract Cost

where:

Capital Intensity Rate:

Posted in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Annex 5.1, is applied to the Total Acceptable Contract Costs to estimate the fixed capital employed for contracts with lower levels of fixed capital investments. This rate is established annually based on a review of fixed capital employed calculations on a sample of contracts to best reflect a lower level of capital intensity.

Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead:

This rate is a 3-year average of the combination of the government marketable bond over 10-year rate with CPI inflation rate 6–10 years ahead.

Total Acceptable Contract Cost:

The base against which the Capital Intensity Rate and Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate with CPI inflation rate 6–10 years are applied.

Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed

Tier 3 is a full schedule approach.

Tier 3 is used for:

Contracts with total estimated or acceptable contract costs greater than $1,000,000

Contracts that do not meet the criteria for Tier 1 and Tier 2

Tier 1 or Tier 2 contracts upon the offeror’s/contractor’s request

The Tier 3 formula is:

Return on Fixed Capital Employed = ( Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead ) × Fixed Capital Employed

where:

Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead:

A 3-year average of the combination of the government marketable bond over 10-year rate with CPI inflation rate 6–10 years ahead.

Fixed Capital Employed:

The percentage of the contractor's fixed capital to be used in executing the contract. It is determined by calculating the allocation of the portion of the net book value of assets to be employed in the contract performance by applying the applicable overhead recovery rates (overhead recovery base / total budget amount of recovery base) for the contract to the applicable assets, as detailed in the Profit Determination Template in the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Annex 5.2. This allocation methodology is required unless a contract represents 100% of an organization’s fixed capital use.

When the contract period extends over more than one of the contractor's fiscal years, the determination of fixed capital employed must be done for each fiscal year within the contract period. The process outlined above applies for the contractor’s first fiscal year in the contract. To determine the fixed capital employed in subsequent years, the following process applies:

the contractor’s net book value of applicable fixed assets is estimated for each fiscal year subsequent to the first year of the contract

the estimated overhead recovery factors for each fiscal year, determined via the contract costing rate negotiations are applied for the allocation of the capital assets

the fixed capital employed for the contract is the sum of the fixed capital employed for each fiscal year of the contractor within the duration of the contract

the government marketable bond over 10-year rate with CPI inflation rate 6-10 years ahead is applied to the total fixed capital employed in the contract

General business risk

General business risk recognizes the contractor’s level of effort and its responsibility and risk in managing the resources required to execute the contract efficiently and economically.

The return on general business risk, along with the level of effort, responsibility and risk, varies with the nature of the costs and the different cost elements of a contract. This is reflected in the following rates of profit to be applied to the total actual or estimated costs of each element below:

Cost elements and rates of profit | Columns: Cost element | Percentage

Cost element: Direct materials | Percentage: 1.5%

Cost element: Subcontracts | Percentage: 2%

Cost element: Accountable advance spares embodied | Percentage: 2%

Cost element: Direct labour | Percentage: 4%

Cost element: Overhead | Percentage: 4%

Cost element: All other allowable costs | Percentage: 1.5%

Cost element: Pass through costs | Percentage: 0%

The costs for each element is multiplied by its associated percentage. The sum of these calculated amounts equals the total general business risk profit.

For more on General Business Risk, including cost element definition, intention, examples, calculations, see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2, Negotiated Profit Element: General Business Risk.

For guidance on calculating rates of profit for General Business Risk, refer to the Profit Determination Template in Annex 5.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing .

Contractual risk

Contractual risk represents the likelihood of financial loss to the contractor due to contract-specific factors. Assessing contractual risk involves understanding the risks present within the contract and understanding which party is best to carry the risk for the various components of the contract. The level of profit on contractual risk is based on how much of the risk is carried by the contractor. A higher profit rate for contractual risk, is the result of the risk being assigned to and borne by the contractor. It is important to ensure the assignment of risk is clear, transparent and well-defined.

As shown in the table below, the degree of financial risk taken on by a contractor is dependent on the contract basis of payment, which determines the standard (the lowest rate in the range) and the available range of profit:

Contractor financial risk based on payment type | Columns: Basis of payment | Standard rate | Maximum rate

Basis of payment: Fixed price or firm price | Standard rate: 4% | Maximum rate: 7%

Basis of payment: Fixed or firm rate type: ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 4.5%

Basis of payment: Fixed or firm rate type: No ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 3.5%

Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with fixed fee: ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 4.5%

Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with fixed fee: No ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 0% | Maximum rate: 1%

Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 4.5%

Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with no fee or fee based on actual costs | Standard rate: 0% | Maximum rate: 0%

Note : The percentage can be lower than the standard rate if the nature of the work involves little to no risk.

Once the standard contractual risk profit rate is determined, the level of contractual risk profit that varies within the ranges depends on the presence, likelihood and impact of the following key risk factors:

Accuracy of costing : which affects the risk that the costing estimates are not accurate and the contract will cost more than planned, and includes:

how well the scope, requirements and demand are established

the reliability of cost estimates

potential fluctuations from external factors

the timing of negotiations

Technical and schedule factors : which affect the risk that contract-specific factors and requirements will impact a contractor’s ability to successfully complete the contract, and include:

technical risk

familiarity and program (products/services/processes/tasks) maturity

performance specifications

contract duration

Justification must be provided for the total contractual risk profit percentage. You may use the Contractual Risk Assessment Tool to calculate the contractual risk profit percentage and document decisions.

The contractual risk profit rate is applied to the total estimated contract costs to develop the contract price. The contract risk profit formula is:

Total Contractual Risk Profit = Contractual Risk Rate % × Total Contract Costs

For more on Contractual Risk, including definitions, intension, examples and calculations, see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2.3, Negotiated Profit Element: Contractual Risk.

Tier 1 is an optional, simplified approach that can only be used for contracts with total estimated or acceptable contract costs less than or equal to $1,000,000.

The Tier 1 formula is:

Return on Working Capital Employed = 1-year Guaranteed Investment Certificate (GIC) Rate × Total Acceptable Contract Cost

where:

1-Year GIC Rate :

A 3-year rolling average of the 1-Year GIC Rate is applied in Tier 1 Working Capital Employed calculations.

This rate, sourced from the Bank of Canada, can be found in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table in Annex 5.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing

If profit determination is related to previous periods, applicable rates during the same periods must be used

The following clause must be included in the price proposal, after consultation with the Price Advisory Group:

“The price quoted includes an amount of profit using the 3-year rolling average 1-Year GIC Rate of (insert appropriate rate) percent. In the event that the annual 1-Year GIC Rate at the time of contract award has changed by more than one full point, up or down from the previous year, after consultation with the Price Advisory Group, the price will be adjusted to reflect the applicable rate.”

Total Acceptable Contract Cost:

The total estimated acceptable contract costs applied as the base against which the 3-year average GIC rate is applied, representing the maximum working capital a contractor would have employed in the contract.

Tier 2 is a full schedule approach for contracts with total estimated or acceptable contract costs over $1,000,000. It can also be used for Tier 1 contracts upon request from the offeror/contractor.

Refer to Tier 2 - Working Capital tab of the Profit Determination Template for more information.

The Tier 2 formula is:

Return on Working Capital Employed = Bank Prime Rate / 12 × Sum of Cumulative Monthly Working Capital Employed

where:

Bank Prime Rate / 12:

The 3-year average bank prime rate applicable to the contract for the Tier 2 Working Capital calculation.

This rate, sourced from the Bank of Canada, can be found in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table in Annex 5.1 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing

If profit determination is related to previous periods, applicable rates during the same periods must be used

In the event that the relevant rates at the time of contract award have changed by more than one full point, up or down, the return will be recomputed applying the revised rates

You must consult with the Price Advisory Group to ensure that the appropriate clause is included in the price proposal

Cumulative Working Capital Employed:

Contractors must submit a schedule of estimated monthly net working capital for the contract, using the Return on Working Capital Employed Tab found in the Profit Determination Template in the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing . In order to prepare and review this schedule, the following information is required:

Monthly Estimated Net Working Capital:

The schedule of acceptable contract costs, exclusive of depreciation (per the contract clauses and conditions, Contract Cost Principles 1031-2 and the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing Annex 2, Costing Standard)

The schedule of contract revenue payments, exclusive of profit (payment plan/schedule)

Advance payments must be applied in the full schedule

Contracts with advance payments and progress payments do not require a return on working capital employed

Calculation methodology: The 3-year average bank prime rate, as defined above, is an annual rate of return. The calculation below accumulates the monthly working capital return, and as such, the 3-year average bank prime rate is divided by 12 to reflect the monthly earning. The return on the cumulative working capital employed is calculated as follows:

Total : Bank Prime Rate / 12 × Monthly Cumulative Working Capital

Return on fixed capital employed

Fixed capital employed consists of the investments contractors make that the company will use for more than one year. Fixed costs are typically purchased for use in the current and future years.

The investment in fixed capital is capitalized, allowing the contractor to spread out the cost over the asset's useful life. Contractors are required to invest in fixed capital for the purposes of carrying out contracts and the fixed capital can often be used for one or multiple contracts.

For more calculations, considerations and examples for Return on Fixed Capital Employed, refer to the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2.1.2, Return on Fixed Capital Employed.

The provision of a return on fixed capital employed is intended not only to compensate contractors for the cost of money associated with the fixed capital employed on the contract, but also to encourage investment in new capital equipment, the result of which is generally greater productivity and consequently reduced costs to Canada.

For the purpose of this section, the fixed capital employed is defined as the net book value of fixed assets, less:

Tier 1 is a simplified, optional approach for all contracts with total estimated or acceptable costs less than or equal to $1,000,000. The Tier 1 formula is:

Return on Fixed Capital Employed = 1% × Total Acceptable Contract Cost

If machinery and/or equipment owned by the contractor is regularly used in the manufacture of the product(s), or in the provision of services, under the contract, a return on fixed capital employed of 1% of total allowable costs will be awarded.

Tier 2 return on fixed capital employed

Tier 2 is a simplified, optional approach for:

Contracts with total estimated or acceptable costs less than or equal to $20,000,000

Contracts with lower fixed capital intensity level, such as services contracts with minimal fixed capital needs

Tier 1 contracts upon the offeror’s/contractor’s request

The Tier 2 formula is:

Return on Fixed Capital Employed = Capital Intensity Rate × ( Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index (CPI) Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead ) × Total Acceptable Contract Cost

where:

Capital Intensity Rate:

Posted in the Applicable Rates for Profit Determination Table the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Annex 5.1, is applied to the Total Acceptable Contract Costs to estimate the fixed capital employed for contracts with lower levels of fixed capital investments. This rate is established annually based on a review of fixed capital employed calculations on a sample of contracts to best reflect a lower level of capital intensity.

Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead:

This rate is a 3-year average of the combination of the government marketable bond over 10-year rate with CPI inflation rate 6–10 years ahead.

Total Acceptable Contract Cost:

The base against which the Capital Intensity Rate and Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate with CPI inflation rate 6–10 years are applied.

Tier 3 return on fixed capital employed

Tier 3 is a full schedule approach.

Tier 3 is used for:

Contracts with total estimated or acceptable contract costs greater than $1,000,000

Contracts that do not meet the criteria for Tier 1 and Tier 2

Tier 1 or Tier 2 contracts upon the offeror’s/contractor’s request

The Tier 3 formula is:

Return on Fixed Capital Employed = ( Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead ) × Fixed Capital Employed

where:

Marketable Bond Over 10-Year Rate + Consumer Price Index Inflation Rate Forecast 6–10 Year Ahead:

A 3-year average of the combination of the government marketable bond over 10-year rate with CPI inflation rate 6–10 years ahead.

Fixed Capital Employed:

The percentage of the contractor's fixed capital to be used in executing the contract. It is determined by calculating the allocation of the portion of the net book value of assets to be employed in the contract performance by applying the applicable overhead recovery rates (overhead recovery base / total budget amount of recovery base) for the contract to the applicable assets, as detailed in the Profit Determination Template in the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Annex 5.2. This allocation methodology is required unless a contract represents 100% of an organization’s fixed capital use.

When the contract period extends over more than one of the contractor's fiscal years, the determination of fixed capital employed must be done for each fiscal year within the contract period. The process outlined above applies for the contractor’s first fiscal year in the contract. To determine the fixed capital employed in subsequent years, the following process applies:

the contractor’s net book value of applicable fixed assets is estimated for each fiscal year subsequent to the first year of the contract

the estimated overhead recovery factors for each fiscal year, determined via the contract costing rate negotiations are applied for the allocation of the capital assets

the fixed capital employed for the contract is the sum of the fixed capital employed for each fiscal year of the contractor within the duration of the contract

the government marketable bond over 10-year rate with CPI inflation rate 6-10 years ahead is applied to the total fixed capital employed in the contract

General business risk

General business risk recognizes the contractor’s level of effort and its responsibility and risk in managing the resources required to execute the contract efficiently and economically.

The return on general business risk, along with the level of effort, responsibility and risk, varies with the nature of the costs and the different cost elements of a contract. This is reflected in the following rates of profit to be applied to the total actual or estimated costs of each element below:

Cost elements and rates of profit | Columns: Cost element | Percentage

Cost element: Direct materials | Percentage: 1.5%

Cost element: Subcontracts | Percentage: 2%

Cost element: Accountable advance spares embodied | Percentage: 2%

Cost element: Direct labour | Percentage: 4%

Cost element: Overhead | Percentage: 4%

Cost element: All other allowable costs | Percentage: 1.5%

Cost element: Pass through costs | Percentage: 0%

The costs for each element is multiplied by its associated percentage. The sum of these calculated amounts equals the total general business risk profit.

For more on General Business Risk, including cost element definition, intention, examples, calculations, see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2, Negotiated Profit Element: General Business Risk.

For guidance on calculating rates of profit for General Business Risk, refer to the Profit Determination Template in Annex 5.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing .

Contractual risk

Contractual risk represents the likelihood of financial loss to the contractor due to contract-specific factors. Assessing contractual risk involves understanding the risks present within the contract and understanding which party is best to carry the risk for the various components of the contract. The level of profit on contractual risk is based on how much of the risk is carried by the contractor. A higher profit rate for contractual risk, is the result of the risk being assigned to and borne by the contractor. It is important to ensure the assignment of risk is clear, transparent and well-defined.

As shown in the table below, the degree of financial risk taken on by a contractor is dependent on the contract basis of payment, which determines the standard (the lowest rate in the range) and the available range of profit:

Contractor financial risk based on payment type | Columns: Basis of payment | Standard rate | Maximum rate

Basis of payment: Fixed price or firm price | Standard rate: 4% | Maximum rate: 7%

Basis of payment: Fixed or firm rate type: ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 4.5%

Basis of payment: Fixed or firm rate type: No ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 3.5%

Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with fixed fee: ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 4.5%

Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with fixed fee: No ceiling price or limitation of expenditure | Standard rate: 0% | Maximum rate: 1%

Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with target cost/incentive fee | Standard rate: 1% | Maximum rate: 4.5%

Basis of payment: Cost reimbursable with no fee or fee based on actual costs | Standard rate: 0% | Maximum rate: 0%

Note : The percentage can be lower than the standard rate if the nature of the work involves little to no risk.

Once the standard contractual risk profit rate is determined, the level of contractual risk profit that varies within the ranges depends on the presence, likelihood and impact of the following key risk factors:

Accuracy of costing : which affects the risk that the costing estimates are not accurate and the contract will cost more than planned, and includes:

how well the scope, requirements and demand are established

the reliability of cost estimates

potential fluctuations from external factors

the timing of negotiations

Technical and schedule factors : which affect the risk that contract-specific factors and requirements will impact a contractor’s ability to successfully complete the contract, and include:

technical risk

familiarity and program (products/services/processes/tasks) maturity

performance specifications

contract duration

Justification must be provided for the total contractual risk profit percentage. You may use the Contractual Risk Assessment Tool to calculate the contractual risk profit percentage and document decisions.

The contractual risk profit rate is applied to the total estimated contract costs to develop the contract price. The contract risk profit formula is:

Total Contractual Risk Profit = Contractual Risk Rate % × Total Contract Costs

For more on Contractual Risk, including definitions, intension, examples and calculations, see the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing , Section 5.2.3, Negotiated Profit Element: Contractual Risk.
